description:
You are acting as a backup-operator who must verify that newly written
    backup archives can actually be restored and must collect a simple
    performance benchmark for each restore.

    1.  Test data:
        • Create two independent test datasets named 
          “project_omega” and “project_sigma”.
        • Each dataset must live in its own directory under the fixed path
          /home/user/test_data/
            /home/user/test_data/project_omega
            /home/user/test_data/project_sigma
        • Inside every dataset directory create one single file called
          “filler.bin” that is exactly 10 MiB in size.

    2.  Creating the backups:
        • Create the directory /home/user/backups/ if it does not exist yet.
        • For every dataset produce a **compressed** tar archive in that
          directory using this exact naming convention
               /home/user/backups/project_omega_backup.tar.gz
               /home/user/backups/project_sigma_backup.tar.gz
        • Make sure the archive contains only the directory with its file and
          not any leading parent directories (i.e. extraction should recreate
          project_… directly in the current working directory).

    3.  Verification step – simulate “loss”:
        • After the archives are written, delete the original dataset
          directories from /home/user/test_data/ so that a restore is
          necessary.

    4.  Restoring & benchmarking:
        • Restore (extract) each archive **individually** while measuring the
          elapsed wall-clock seconds that the extraction takes.
        • You may use any standard timing utility available in a typical
          Linux environment, e.g.  “/usr/bin/time -f %e”.
        • Perform the restore into /home/user/test_data/ again so that after
          the operation the directory layout is back to its original state
          (project_omega and project_sigma present with their 10 MiB file).

    5.  Mandatory benchmark log:
        • After both restores finish, create the file
              /home/user/restore_benchmark.log
          with **exactly** the  following format (the automated grader depends
          on the exact key names, the colon, a single space and the order of
          the lines):

              DATASET_NAME: project_omega
              SOURCE_TARBALL: /home/user/backups/project_omega_backup.tar.gz
              BACKUP_SIZE_MB: <integer_size_in_MB>
              RESTORE_TIME_SEC: <seconds_float_with_3_decimals>

              DATASET_NAME: project_sigma
              SOURCE_TARBALL: /home/user/backups/project_sigma_backup.tar.gz
              BACKUP_SIZE_MB: <integer_size_in_MB>
              RESTORE_TIME_SEC: <seconds_float_with_3_decimals>

          Rules for the values:
             – BACKUP_SIZE_MB must be the integer, rounded *down*, size in
               mebibytes (base-2) of the tar.gz file on disk (use any tool
               you like to compute it).
             – RESTORE_TIME_SEC must be the wall-clock seconds reported by
               your timing tool, formatted with exactly three digits after
               the decimal point (e.g. 0.137 or 1.923).

        • There must be a blank line (one “\n”) between the two dataset
          blocks and **no extra blank line** at the very end of the file.

    6.  Final state that the automated tests will check:
        • /home/user/test_data/project_omega/filler.bin          exists
        • /home/user/test_data/project_sigma/filler.bin          exists
        • /home/user/backups/project_omega_backup.tar.gz         exists
        • /home/user/backups/project_sigma_backup.tar.gz         exists
        • /home/user/restore_benchmark.log                       exists and
          follows the exact format described above.

    Notes / Hints (not enforced, just guidance):
        • Creating a 10 MiB file can quickly be done with “dd” or “truncate”.
        • To obtain the size of a file in MiB you might divide the byte size
          by 2^20 and cast to an integer.
        • Feel free to use temporary working directories, but remember the
          final locations listed above must exist when you are done.
        • You do *not* need root privileges; stay entirely in /home/user.
truth:
Initial conditions provided by the grader before the agent starts:
        • The directories /home/user/ and /home/user/backups/ already exist
          and are writable by the agent.
        • No project_omega / project_sigma directories or backup archives are
          present yet; the agent is expected to create everything from
          scratch.

    Verification details the grader will use:
        • It will read /home/user/restore_benchmark.log, split it into two
          blocks separated by a single blank line and confirm:
              1. The first block begins with
                 “DATASET_NAME: project_omega”
              2. The second block begins with
                 “DATASET_NAME: project_sigma”
              3. Each block contains exactly four lines, each line matches the
                 KEY: VALUE pattern with the keys shown above, in that order.
              4. BACKUP_SIZE_MB is a positive integer.
              5. RESTORE_TIME_SEC matches the regex ^[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$

        • The grader will untar each *_backup.tar.gz into a temporary dir to
          ensure:
              – The archive root directory is exactly project_omega/ or
                project_sigma/ (no leading “home/user/…” path).
              – Inside that directory there is exactly one file named
                filler.bin and its size is 10485760 bytes (10 MiB).

        • Filesystem presence:
              - /home/user/backups/project_omega_backup.tar.gz exists
              - /home/user/backups/project_sigma_backup.tar.gz exists
              - /home/user/test_data/project_omega/filler.bin exists
              - /home/user/test_data/project_sigma/filler.bin exists

        • Permissions: regular user read/write suffices; no root needed.

    Any deviation from the required log format or missing items causes the
    test to fail.
